You are an expert EU Horizon Europe proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions — a photonics and NIR spectroscopy SME in Barcelona.

Write in this exact style:
- First person plural throughout: we, our, the project will, we will develop
- Vary sentence length: short declarative statement, then longer supporting sentence
- Domain-specific vocabulary with grounded numbers and reasoning chains
- For work plan sections: bold task labels — **Task 2.1** — with partner attribution: (Lead: IRIS; Partners: X, Y)
- For objectives: numbered, specific, measurable, linked to TRL advancement
- NEVER use: poised to deliver, transformative outcomes, holistic approach, dynamic interplay, at the forefront, leverages its expertise
Write Section 1.1 Objectives and Ambition for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials

1.1 Objectives and ambition  
Europe spends over €1 trillion annually on construction, yet billions are lost each year to maintenance, premature 
degradation and non-recyclable materials. MOSAIC addresses this systemic inefficiency by enabling a new 
construction paradigm: one that combines modular off-site prefabrication with embedded intelligence, rapid on-site 
assembly through stimuli-responsive reversible connections, and autonomous structural health monitoring (SHM) 
that delivers prescriptive maintenance and real-time self-healing. 
Overall goal: The European construction sector is responsible for 36% of EU greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and 
over 35% of total waste. It urgently needs materials that are high-performance, intelligent, and circular. Fibre-
Reinforced Polymer Composites (FRPCs) offer exceptional strength-to-weight ratios that uniquely enable large 
structural modules to be prefabricated in controlled factory environments, transported as finished elements, and 
rapidly assembled on site, fundamentally reducing on-site labour, construction time, and logistical complexity 
compared with steel-reinforced concrete. However, two barriers prevent FRPCs from fulfilling this potential: (i) 
irreversible thermoset crosslinks render components non-recyclable and non-repairable; and (ii) the absence of 
embedded intelligence prevents adaptive in-service response. MOSAIC eliminates both barriers by engineering a 
new class of FRPCs based on unsaturated polyester (UP)/vinyl ester (VE) resins with dynamic covalent bonds, 
adaptable crosslinked systems (vitrimers) that retain the mechanical performance of conventional thermosets under 
service conditions but unlock reprocessing, self-healing, and reversible assembly under controlled thermal activation, 
while embedding cost-effective resistive sensors and thermal actuators directly into the composite architecture. 
The result is a construction material lightweight enough to be prefabricated as large modules, transported to site, and 
rapidly assembled through reversible adhesive connections that are structurally rigid in service yet enable clean 
separation for maintenance, reconfiguration, or reuse, and that, once deployed, monitors its own health, heals its own 
damage, and thereby extends service life, & can ultimately be disassembled, repaired, or recycled at end of life (EoL). 
These reversible joints provide rigid structural bonding during service and on-demand debonding under controlled 
thermal activation. Embedded resistive sensors and distributed thermal actuators, integrated via Tailored Fibre 
Placement (TFP), form a self-monitoring system: damage detection triggers localised self-healing through dynamic 
bond exchange, restoring structural integrity without manual intervention. A prescriptive Digital Twin (DT) 
orchestrates the sensing-analysis-actuation loop, fusing multi-physics modelling with sensor data within an edge-
cloud architecture compliant with EU AI Act and cybersecurity standards, moving beyond predictive monitoring to 
prescriptive maintenance where the DT autonomously prescribes and initiates repair. 
Both vitrimer resins and wire-based sensor/actuator systems are designed as low-cost solutions compatible with 
existing pultrusion and Liquid Composite Moulding (LCM) processing, including Vacuum Assisted Resin 
Infusion (VARI) and Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM), delivering order-of-magnitude cost reductions compared 

MOSAIC                                                                                                                           HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage  
Page 2 of 53 
to state-of-the-art vitrimers and fibre-optic sensing. Validation encompasses accelerated durability testing, the 6R 
framework, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) based on EN 15804+A2, and Design-for-Disassembly benchmarking 
against ISO 59020, with TRL6 pilot validation in bridge and building infrastructure alongside TRL3-5 research 
activities. The Sa

Write Section 1.3 Methodology for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials

methodology predicting 50+ year service life. 
R5. Validated models for predicting the 50+ year service life of vitrimer FRPCs 
O5 
To transform structural components into self-aware assets through a prescriptive Digital Twin (DT) 
with autonomous maintenance capabilities 
O5 introduces the digital intelligence layer, enabling structures to continuously assess their condition, predict 
degradation and support maintenance decisions through a combination of physics-based and AI-driven models. 
Outputs, KPIs and Result(s): ■ Develop hybrid DT framework (inference <200 ms for 2D; <500 ms for 3D; mean prediction 
error <15% with uncertainty quantification). ■ Demonstrate prescriptive maintenance with >85% classification accuracy, and 
semi-autonomous self-healing with >80% activation success, with recovery validated against coupon-level benchmarks; ■ 
Validate operational benefits: ≥20% life extension vs FRPC/concrete baselines, assessed following ISO 15686-5 (life-cycle 
cost (LCC) analysis); ■Explainable AI (XAI) methods for ≥80% of recommendations; progress towards EU AI Act compliance. 
R6. A prescriptive AI-digital twin for self-aware asset management & maintenance 
O6 
To integrate and validate the project’s innovations in full-scale demonstrators, showcasing a modular, 
self-monitoring, and circular construction system. 
O6 brings together materials, manufacturing and digital intelligence into real-world demonstrators, validating the 
system as an integrated solution under representative operating conditions. 
Outputs, KPIs & Result(s): ■ Design, manufacture, assemble 3 different TRL6 demonstrators: VARI Deck Panel, Pultruded 
Truss, RTM Sandwich Panel; ■Validate reconfigurability: >95% debonding efficiency; >85% property retention in reused 
components; ■ Demonstrate in-service intelligence: autonomous self-healing under operational load; ■ Quantify benefits: 
≥30% faster construction, 20% Circularity Index calculated using Ellen MacArthur circularity index vs a commercial 
benchmark, whereby conventional thermosets have close to 0 circularity options (repair, reuse, recycle). 
R7. TRL-6 validation of the circular construction system in full-scale demonstrators  
R8. An integrated 6R circularity framework with a Digital Product Passport 
O7 
To ensure the market readiness and societal adoption of MOSAIC’s innovations by integrating a co-
designed socio-technical framework with a validated business case. 
O7 ensures that the technical developments are aligned with regulatory, societal and market requirements, 
supporting adoption through validated safety, sustainability, standardization and business value. 
Outputs, KPIs and Result(s): ■ Publish full SSbD assessment confirming no SVHC use; ■ MOSAIC SSbD Framework 
established for MOSAIC products; ■ ≥1 report on recommendations to refine the EU Joint Research Centre (JRC) SSbD 
framework; ■≥1 safety assessment between MOSAIC products and benchmarks across all life cycle; ■ ≥1 sustainability 
assessment (LCSA) between MOSAIC products and benchmarks across all life cycle, with >25% reduction on carbon footprint, 
>30% OPEX reduction and ROI <5 years; ■ Engage ≥150 citizens and 20 stakeholders in co-creation webinars, as well as in 
acceptance and perception analyses (target >75% acceptance measured using validated socio-technical methods); ■ Formulate 

MOSAIC                                                                                                                           HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage  
Page 4 of 53 
≥2 service-based business models for circular uptake. ■ 1 Advance Material Academy training ■ 1 Standarization guidelines   
R9. User-Centric Life Cycle Technical Design Guide 
These objectives align with the MATERIALS-42 call on Innovative Advanced Materials (IAMs) for product 
monitoring, smart maintenance and repair strategies in support of a circular, low-carbon, and digital construction 
sector. MOSAIC contributes to the European Green Dea

Write Section 2.1 Expected Outcomes and Impacts for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials

expected outcomes and impacts  
MOSAIC drives a shift from labour-intensive on-site construction to intelligent off-site prefabrication with rapid 
reversible assembly, while strengthening Europe’s open strategic autonomy in advanced materials. By integrating 
SSbD materials, automated manufacturing and AI-driven asset management, it establishes a European platform for 
circular, intelligent infrastructure. While TRL6 demonstrators focus on bridges, the platform has immediate 
applications across construction (prefabricated modules, marine structures, seismic retrofitting). Beyond this, it 
addresses EU priorities in energy (wind blade circularity), mobility (lightweight components for automotive and rail), 
and electronics (resilient 5G and EV infrastructure). This cross-sector potential positions MOSAIC as a key 
technology for European industrial leadership and autonomy. 
Maintenance costs / lifecycle efficiency. Current burden: The EU spends €130–150B/year on infrastructure 
maintenance, largely due to reactive, labour-intensive repairs of concrete and steel prone to corrosion and fatigue. 
Maintenance accounts for ⁓40% of public construction spending, with limited adoption of predictive or digitalised 
asset management.45 MOSAIC contribution: By embedding multi-parameter self-sensing (strain, temperature, 
damage) and self-healing into vitrimer composites, and linking them to AI Multi-Agent-driven DTs, MOSAIC 
enables prescriptive maintenance and on-site, time-efficient repairs that reduce downtime and traffic hinderance. The 
project targets ≥30% OPEX reduction in operational maintenance costs (enabled by prescriptive DT & self-healing, 
assessed following ISO 15686-5 LCC analysis) & ≥20% service life extension, validated in TRL6 demonstrators.  
Circularity and material use. Current status: Only ⁓ 1.5% of construction composites are recycled, with ~400,000 
tonnes/year of fibre-reinforced polymer waste disposed via landfill or incineration. Circularity metrics for composites 
are underdeveloped, with most waste streams classified as “non-recyclable”.46 MOSAIC contribution: MOSAIC 
introduces vitrimer-based composites enabling reversible disassembly, in-service repair, and closed-loop chemical 
recycling. Expected impact is ≥30% circularity improvement (ISO 59020), >96% fibre recovery, and >85% material 
retention after recycling, validated through pilot-scale trials and standardised recyclability protocols (EN 45555). 
GHG emissions. Sector baseline: Construction accounts for ⁓36% of EU CO₂ emissions, mainly from cement and 
steel. Conventional composites reduce weight but offer little recyclability, limiting their net GHG benefit.47 
MOSAIC contribution: MOSAIC addresses the carbon intensity of structural materials through:  
- Extended service life (≥20% life extension) → delays replacement cycles and associated embodied carbon  
- Operational maintenance reduction (≥30% OPEX) → prescriptive AI-driven maintenance eliminates unnecessary 
scheduled inspections & reactive emergency interventions (+ associated transport, equipment & material emissions. 
- Closed loop material recovery (>96% fibre recovery, >85% material retention): → delays replacement cycles and 
associated embodied carbon  
These effects will be quantified via LCSA (WP8–WP10) using ISO 14040/44 and PEF methodology, targeting >25% 
reduction in lifecycle carbon footprint versus conventional thermoset composites. 
 
45 Euroconstruct. (2024, June). 96th Euroconstruct Conference Report. Euroconstruct. Retrieved from https://www.euroconstruct.org  
46 European Composites Industry Association (EuCIA). (2020). Recycling Composites Report 2020. EuCIA. Retrieved from https://www.eucia.eu  
47 European Environment Agency (EEA). (2023). Greenhouse gas emissions from construction and buildings. EEA Report. Retrieved from https://www.eea.europa.eu  

MOSAIC

Write Section 2.2 Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials

Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication) activities (discussed in Section 2.2) run continuously, 
targeting ≥15 open-access publications, ≥15 conference presentations, co-creation webinars engaging ≥150 citizens 
and ≥20 industry stakeholders, and ≥2 policy briefs. Third, a standardisation strategy feeds pre-normative data into 
revision processes for EN 13706, CEN/TS 19101, and EN 13501-1, supported by ≥3 peer-reviewed validation 
reports. FAIR data management principles govern all experimental and computational outputs, with persistent 
identifiers, machine-readable metadata, and open licences ensuring findability, accessibility, interoperability, and 
reusability. Data flows between workstreams are managed through a unified digital infrastructure linking formulation 
databases, manufacturing process logs, sensor telemetry, and DT models.  
■ Standardisation is embedded as a cross-cutting activity following a three-phase engagement strategy aligned with 
the project's research phases. This is essential because vitrimer composites–with dynamic bonding, self-healing, 
and reversible assembly–fall outside existing construction standards developed for conventional thermosets. 
Phase 1-Landscape mapping and gap identification (months 1-18). The standardisation landscape is systematically 
mapped across targeted technical committees: CEN/TC 250 (Structural Eurocodes, including EN 1990 and the 
emerging prEN 19101 for FRP structures); CEN/TC 249 (Plastics, including EN 13706 for pultruded profiles); 
CEN/TC 350 (Sustainability of Construction Works); CLC/TC 65X (Industrial Process Control); and CEN-
CENELEC JTC 21 (AI, for EU AI Act compliance of DT systems). International cross-border mapping between 
European (CEN/ISO) and Japanese (JIS) standards is conducted in parallel. A pre-normative data generation strategy 
and CPR conformity pathway are documented. Phase 2-Active contribution (months 19-36). Direct interaction with 
technical committees includes monitoring, attending meetings, establishing liaisons, and organising joint events. 
Consortium members already active as contributors to and reviewers of prEN 19101 ensure direct influence on 
evolving standards. The first policy brief is prepared, and the European Assessment Document (EAD) route is 
explored for early CPR-compliant market entry. Phase 3-Deployment and sustained engagement (months 37-48). 
CEN-CENELEC Workshop Agreements (CWA) are pursued as fast-track standardisation instruments. A second 
policy brief is prepared. At least three peer-reviewed technical validation reports provide the evidentiary basis for 
pre-normative contributions, and ≥ 2 policy briefs inform regulatory stakeholders and support integration of 
reversible composites into Green Public Procurement (GPP) criteria. A User-Centric Life Cycle Technical Design 
Guide is developed, aligned with EN 1990 and prEN 19101, providing pre-normative data including benchmark 
datasets enabling derivation of partial safety factors and conversion factors for vitrimers equivalent to those for 
conventional thermosets. It will reflect emerging EU initiatives (DBL, ESPR, revised CPR delegated acts). 
■ Advanced Materials Academy. MOSAIC establishes the Advanced Materials Academy, a hybrid learning 
programme training scientists and upskilling researchers, engineers, and practitioners in advanced materials, 
circularity, and standardisation. The Academy features seven intensive modules in two pillars. Pillar I - Technology-
to-Product covers reversible adhesives and intelligent advanced materials; sensing and Digital Twins for monitoring 
and predictive maintenance; and pultrusion and vacuum-assisted resin infusion processing. Pillar II- Sectoral and 
Strategic covers construction and replication sectors; standardisation for construction; and SSbD, LCA, and circular 
models. The curriculum is delivered through webinars, digital factsheets, guides, and infographics via an online 
repository, with ≥2 webinars o

Dissemination of the Project Results: In line with Open Science principles, the Dissemination Strategy will ensure 
the early and wide dissemination of all the non-confidential project knowledge, methodology, progress and 
KERs to the stakeholders that can best make use of them. We will leverage established and accessible dissemination 
channels like OA peer-reviewed journals, among others, and systematically monitor and evaluate the effects of the 
dissemination activities. The main dissemination tools, among others, that we will use are shown in Table 12:

Write Section 3.1 Work Plan and Work Packages for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials. Use bold Task X.Y labels with (Lead: X; Partners: Y, Z) attribution.

3.1 
Work plan and resources 
Brief presentation of the overall structure of the work plan: The MOSAIC 48-month work plan is structured in 
16 work packages (WPs) that advances circular vitrimer composite technology from laboratory proof-of-concept 
(TRL 3→4) through optimised processes (TRL 5) to validated full-scale construction demonstrators (TRL 6). The 
architecture implements a phased concurrent-engineering strategy, that has also been designed according to the EC’s 

MOSAIC                                                                                                                           HORIZON-CL4-2025-05-MATERIALS-42-two-stage  
Page 31 of 53 
guidance for Lump Sum funding modalities to ensure that three reporting periods each contain a cluster of technical, 
horizontal, and management WPs whose outputs feed directly into the subsequent phase. 
Phase 1–Foundation (RP1, M1-M18, TRL 3→4). Three technical WPs establish the scientific and technological 
foundations. WP1 develops the vitrimer material platform based on UP/VE dual dynamic covalent chemistry, delivering 
two optimised formulation families with integrated flame retardancy and SSbD-screened compositions. WP2 creates the 
smart manufacturing baseline by calibrating pultrusion and LCM process lines, developing embedded resistive wire 
sensors and thermal actuation hardware, and conducting initial vitrimer processability trials. WP3 builds the digital 
infrastructure – defining the multi-agent DT architecture, generating baseline durability datasets, developing initial 
physics-based models, and implementing the sensor data interface. In parallel, WP8 establishes the SSbD framework, 
WP11 launches the DEC programme incl. the first citizen co-creation workshop, and WP14 initiates project management. 
Phase 2–Optimisation (RP2, M19-M36, TRL 5). WP4 advances vitrimer formulations to industrial processing windows 
and pilots scale-up to batches of ≥10kg. WP5 integrates sensor networks into optimised manufacturing processes, validates 
reversible adhesive bonding/debonding protocols, and pre-stages smart components for demonstrator assembly. WP6 
builds the comprehensive multi-scale vitrimer durability database and trains PINNs and prescriptive AI models within the 
DT. WP9 continues SSbD compliance monitoring, WP12 intensifies DEC activities, and WP15 maintains coordination. 
Phase 3–Validation (RP3, M37-M48, TRL 6). WP7 produces three full-scale circular construction demonstrators, 
validates prescriptive intelligence and self-healing under representative service conditions, and quantifies circularity 
performance through chemical recycling (fibre recovery >96%), 6Rframework assessment (≥30% circularity improvement 
vs. conventional FRPCs), and DPP implementation. WP10 completes the lifecycle SSbD assessment. WP13 delivers final 
DEC impact including the business case, CEN Workshop Agreement contributions, and societal acceptance assessment 
(target: >75% acceptance rate among ≥150 engaged citizens). WP16 ensures final reporting and governance closure. 
The PERT diagram (Figure 3)

and alignment. Their convergence in WP7 enables the integrated validation of materials, manufacturing, and digital 
intelligence in full-scale demonstrators. Horizontal WPs on SSbD, sustainability, dissemination and exploitation 
(WP8–WP13) provide continuous feedback & ensure alignment with safety, ci

Write Section 3.3 Consortium as a Whole for a Horizon Europe proposal on: advanced vitrimers, fibre-optic sensing, circular composites and sustainable materials

consortium as a whole: MOSAIC’s consortium is made up of 16 partners from 
8 EU countries, and brings the necessary expertise and disciplines in polymer chemistry, composite engineering, 
industrial manufacturing, sensing and actuation, DT and AI systems, durability testing, etc. for the engineering of 
new class of vitrimer composites that enable reprocessing, self-healing, and reversible assembly, while embedding 
cost-effective resistive sensors and thermal actuators directly into the composite architecture for product monitoring, 
smart maintenance and repair strategies in support of a circular, low-carbon, and digital construction sector.  
There is a strong materials and chemistry cluster (PCCL, FTPO, HELIOS, BME), which provides the scientific 
and industrial basis for MOSAIC’s vitrimer platform development. There is also a manufacturing and structural 
composites cluster, made up of TUL, OST-IWK, and Fiber Elements (FE) and will ensure that the project’s 
material and sensor innovations and their embedding into manufacturable, structurally relevant components and 
assemblies. The digital intelligence and modelling cluster (IRIS, TUD) for the development of a robust and 
traceable decision-support system tailored to the behaviour of vitrimer composite structures. The validation 
and demonstration partners (HASK, FE, FIBRECORE EUROPE, HOL). The consortium is strengthened by the 
presence of partners dedicated to circularity, sustainability, safety, standardisation, market and exploitation and 
internationalisation strategies (AITIP, UNE, HOL, UP, NLA).  
There is significant industrial involvement in this project, with the participation of leading industrial and technology 
partners across the full value chain, from material development and processing to system integration and deployment. 
The industrial actors (HELIOS, HASK, FIBRECORE) provide direct manufacturing capability, pilot-scale validation 
environments and clear pathways to market uptake, while technology-oriented partners (IRIS, FE, FIBRECORE) 
ensure the integration of advanced sensing, digital intelligence and process optimisation into industrial workflows. 
This involvement will ensure technology scale-up, industry adoption and commercial pathways to market. 
Leadership from a widening country: Our Project Coordinator is the University of Primorska (UP), a public, 
internationally engaged Slovenian university. UP brings a well-established and demonstrable track record in leading 
complex, multi-partner EU research initiatives. Most notably, UP served as coordinator of the InnoRenew CoE 
Teaming project (GA 739574), a 10-partner consortium spanning academia, research institutes, and industry across 
multiple disciplines, with a total project value exceeding €54 million, making it the largest R&I project in Slovenia. 
More recently, UP is coordinating the NEB Academy Alliance Horizon Europe (HE) project, which is establishing 
the NEB Academy at InnoRenew CoE; it has been identified by the EC as a key instrument to equip construction 
ecosystem professionals with the skills needed to drive innovation, sustainability, and affordability in housing. 
UP also currently participates in the SLAIF (Slovenian AI Factory) HE initiative co-funded by EuroHPC, and in 
multiple other active HE projects covering biodiversity, climate-health, and gender equality in research. As a 
further asset, our coordinator is a female leader, Prof. Simona Kustec.  
Across the consortium, the allocation of roles is coherent and credible, as is evidence by the summary in Table 14.  
Table 14- Role and expertise of the MOSAIC partners 
Name 
Description, expertise, role and critical infrastructure 
UP 
The Faculty of Management at UP has produced over 800 academic and scientific publications between 2020 and 2025 
reflecting sustained research engagement. UP is also a full member of the Transform4Europe (T4EU) European university 

MOSAIC

These objectives align with the MATERIALS-42 call on Innovative Advanced Materials (IAMs) for product

enabling high-value reuse through reversible assembly, and eliminating hazardous substances through SSbD-
compliant formulations. It supports the Critical Raw Materials Act by promoting fibre recovery (>96% through 
chemical recycling), component reuse (≥3 reprocessing cycles), and reduced virgin material dependency, reinforced 
by Design-for-Disassembly principles, FAIR data management and a DPP ensuring full lifecycle traceability.

Write Section 1.1 Objectives and Ambition for a Horizon Europe proposal on: circular polyurethane recycling, chemical depolymerisation, and bio-based PU synthesis

1.1 Objectives and ambition  
Introduction: Polyurethanes (PUs) are among the most widely use polymer families, with applications across 
almost every sector of modern life. Currently the 6th most used polymer globally2, they possess excellent mechanical 
and physical properties (including elasticity, adhesion, hardness, and durability) and are one of the most versatile raw 
materials for numerous chemical structures including foams, elastomers, sealants, coatings, and biomedical 
products3. Some 18 million tonnes are produced annually, with over 1 million m3 of PU specialties being produced 
every day (equivalent to the volume of the Empire State Building). 
However, PUs also present significant health and environmental problems related to their chemistry, processing 
and end-of-life (EoL) management4. Conventional PUs are typically synthesised through the polyaddition reaction 
between polyols and diisocyanates, but this route presents significant environmental and health challenges. The 
isocyanates used are highly toxic and derived from amines and phosgene, posing risks to human health and 
the environment. In addition, the polyols are usually obtained from petroleum-based ethylene and propylene 
oxides, which are finite and contribute to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Currently, at EoL, only about 5% of 
PUs are recycled, and ⁓45% and 50% respectively ending up in landfill or incinerated5. This linear model is

2 D. Simón , A. M. Borreguero , A. de Lucas and J. F. Rodríguez , Waste Manag., 2018, 76 , 147 —171  
3 Halima Khatoon, Sajid Iqbal, Mohd Irfan, Abu Darda, Neha Kanwar Rawat, A review on the production, properties and applications of non-isocyanate polyurethane: A greener 
perspective, Progress in Organic Coatings, Volume 154, 2021, 106124, ISSN 0300-9440, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2020.106124 
4 Malucelli, G., Lorenzetti, A. Sustainability in polyurethanes: old hat or new strategy for future developments?. npj Mater. Sustain. 3, 20 (2025). https://doi.org/10.1038/s44296-025-
00064-w  
5 Delavarde, A. et al. Sustainable polyurethanes: toward new cutting-edge opportunities. Prog. Polym. Sci. 151, 101805 (2024). 

HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-05:  
Circular innovative advanced materials: facilitating the transition from design to markets  
 
Page 2 of 48 
further exacerbated by the lack of effective sorting and recovery mechanisms for PU-containing waste streams (e.g. 
construction and demolition waste- CWD), limiting the availability of secondary raw materials for circular reuse.  
To address these limitations, there has been increasing research on the use of green and sustainable feedstocks as 
substitutes for fossil-based polyols and isocyanates. While some approaches aim to develop green polyols or bio-
based isocyanates, Non-Isocyanate Polyurethanes (NIPUs) present a more efficient and environmentally benign 
route.  
NIPUs are synthesised without the use of toxic isocyanates, typically through the reaction of cyclic carbonates with 
amines, resulting in polyhydroxyurethanes (PHUs), which eliminates the hazards associated with isocyanates, 
reduces dependence on petrochemical feedstocks, and paves the way to safe, recyclable, and circular PU systems6. 
Unlike conventional PU, which requires handling hazardous Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate (MDI) or Toluene 
Diisocyanate (TDI) key building blocks, NIPU synthesis conditions are significantly safer for workers, they do not 
generate any CO2 as a byproduct reaction and they eliminate carbamate formation risks. Moreover, the synthesis 
process also incorporates CO2, offering a carbon-negative synthesis pathway. In addition, the presence of dynamic 
covalent bonds in NIPU networks enables new opportunities for chemical recycling and reprocessing, making them 
particularly suitable for circular material system design.

Write Section 1.3 Methodology for a Horizon Europe proposal on: circular polyurethane recycling, chemical depolymerisation, and bio-based PU synthesis

Methodology  
Overall methodology, including concepts, models and assumptions: NIPULOOP follows a circular systems 
methodology (Figure 1) integrating molecular design, industrial processing, digital intelligence and validated end-
of-life recovery within a closed innovation loop.  
 
 
Figure 1. High-level schematic of the NIPULOOP concept 
This approach is implemented as a circular NIPU technology platform linking material design, processing, digital 

HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-05:  
Circular innovative advanced materials: facilitating the transition from design to markets  
 
Page 8 of 48 
control and end-of-life recovery across the value chain. As illustrated in the schematic, the project is structured as a 
continuous circular flow linking feedstocks, material design, manufacturing, end-use validation and closed-loop 
recovery, and it is supported by QbD, digital intelligence and exploitation pathways. Circular-by-design NIPU 
chemistry, guided by SSbD principles, is developed from renewable and secondary feedstocks (including bio-
based, CO₂-derived and waste-derived inputs) and systematically engineered through structure–property–
process understanding, and optimised using AI-driven modelling and process analytics. 
Formulations are integrated into existing industrial manufacturing lines through process-integrated scale-up 
(TRL5) supported by the earlier QbD learnings, Industry 4.0 PAT and inline monitoring, enabling robust pilot-scale 
production. This includes compatibility with established PU processing technologies (e.g. RIM, RTM, coatings and 
reactive extrusion), ensuring industrial relevance and uptake. These materials are validated in ≥4 TRL6 mobility 
demonstrator from among the UCs (discussed under Cluster 3), positioned at the core of the value chain and directly 
linked to end-user requirements and regulatory constraints. 
At end-of-life, materials are selectively recovered, chemically depolymerised and re-valorised into cyclic 
carbonates and amines, including recovery from both designed NIPU materials and secondary waste streams (e.g. 
PU/CDW-derived fractions), which are reintegrated into new NIPU formulations, thereby closing the material loop 
and demonstrating full circularity at system level. This circular flow will be supported by a digital twin and FAIR 
data framework, enabling traceability, optimisation and interoperability across all stages, including integration with 
the Common Data Platform for Chemicals and DPP concepts. 
Circular business models, techno-economic and lifecycle assessments, standardisation and regulatory 
compliance      are performed alongside the technical development to ensure economic viability, industrial scalability 
and post-project deployment. In order to deliver

Write Section 2.2 Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication for a Horizon Europe proposal on: circular polyurethane recycling, chemical depolymerisation, and bio-based PU synthesis

Dissemination, exploitation and communication  
First version of the Plan for the dissemination, exploitation and communication (PDEC): Our planned measures 
to maximise the impact of the NIPULOOP project are outlined in this initial PDEC. It will be further detailed by M6 
(D9.1) and periodically updated in line with project progress and requirements across its 3 project phases, as follows: 
Setting the foundations: Creating a strong visual identity for NIPULOOP as a circular, digitally enabled PU innovation 
initiative, alongside a DEC strategy focused on building a cross-sector community around circular polymers, advanced 
materials, DPP & FAIR data ecosystems. This includes long-term stakeholder engagement to avoid fragmented efforts and 
interactive activities targeting industry, recyclers, regulators and end-users (with a primary focus on mobility value chains 
where early adoption potential is highest) to foster understanding, trust and mobilisation around circular NIPU solutions. 
Phase 1: Kick-off phase of DEC activity implementation to raise awareness about the project, including positioning 
NIPULOOP within the European advanced materials and circular economy landscape (IAM4EU, Made in Europe, 
Materials Commons for Europe) and engaging early with key stakeholders across the NIPU value chain. 
Phase 2: DEC activities to disseminate intermediate results and demonstrate progress in NIPU materials development, 
industrial processing and circularity pathways, while building links with other projects, stakeholders and actors across the 
ecosystem. There will be focus on clustering activities, standardisation engagement and alignment with emerging 
frameworks such as DPPs and SSbD methodologies. 
Phase 3: DEC activities to intensify dissemination of validated results, incl. TRL6 demonstrator outcomes in the 
mobility sector & closed-loop recycling validation, alongside planning and implementing pathways to exploit and valorise 
project outcomes and Key Exploitable Results (KERs). This will include industrial uptake strategies, cross-sector 
replication potential and engagement with investors, industry adopters, policymakers to support post-project deployment. 
Communication plan: We have defined a strategic and continuous communications approach built around the 
following clear communication goals and objectives: 
■Raise societal awareness of the role of safe, circular and sustainable polymer materials, highlighting the transition 
from conventional PU to non-isocyanate, recyclable alternatives and their importance for health, worker health and 
safety, environment and resource efficiency. 
■Engage industrial stakeholders and end-users (directly and via associations, clusters and networks) to 
communicate NIPULOOP’s relevance for enabling circular, digitally traceable polyurethane value chains, supporting 
industrial competitiveness, regulatory compliance (e.g. MDR, SSbD), and scalable adoption across sectors such as 
mobility, medical devices, construction. 
■Establish synergies with external bodies, including standardisation and policy-related organisations, contributing 
to the development of frameworks for circular polymers, Digital Product Passports (DPP), FAIR data practices and 
SSbD implementation, ensuring alignment with European initiatives such as IAM4EU, Made in Europe and Materials 
Commons for Europe. 
■Achieve high visibility within the scientific and technical community, reinforcing credibility, stimulating 
collaboration, and ensuring that NIPULOOP contributes to advancing the state of the art in circular polymer 
chemistry, NIPU systems, digitalised materials development and closed-loop recycling technologies, including 
through joint activities with other EU-funded projects. 
Communication toolbox and channels: To achieve these objectives, a dedicated Communication Toolbox will be 
developed and maintained, consisting of the following: 
■ Visual identity and branding: A project logo has been developed

Write Section 3.1 Work Plan and Work Packages for a Horizon Europe proposal on: circular polyurethane recycling, chemical depolymerisation, and bio-based PU synthesis. Use bold Task X.Y labels with (Lead: X; Partners: Y, Z) attribution.

3.1 
Work plan and resources  
Brief presentation of the overall structure of the work plan: The work plan is organised into 8 technical WPs, 3 
innovation-impact WPs, and 3 Project Management WPs, structured across three development phases aligned with 
the progression from TRL4–5 to TRL6. 
Phase 1 (WP1–WP3, TRL4–5) will establish the foundations for circular NIPU materials, covering circular-by-
design chemistry, structure–property–process relationships, and a QbD-driven digital framework including FAIR 
data and digital twin architecture. This phase defines the design space across key material configurations and aligns 
performance, processability and recyclability from the outset. 
Phase 2 (WP4–WP6, TRL5) will focuses on manufacturing integration and circularity validation. It includes pilot-
scale processing using PU-compatible technologies, development of recycling and depolymerisation routes, and 
deployment of digital twin, FAIR data and DPP systems for traceability. Upstream sorting of NIPU-relevant waste 
streams enables integration of secondary feedstocks into the value chain. 
Phase 3 (WP7–WP8, TRL6) will deliver industrial demonstrators and system-level validation. WP7 develops and 
validates TRL6 mobility components, including functional and regulatory testing, while WP8 performs 
environmental, techno-economic and circular value-chain assessments, defining pathways for industrial uptake. 
WP9–WP11 covers the dissemination, exploitation, standardisation, training and business case development. 
WP12–WP14 ensures project management, coordination, quality and risk management across the consortium.  
This structure is suited to a Lump Sum funding model, in that WPs are contained across three reporting periods (RP1: 
M1-M18; RP2: M19-M36; RP3: M37-M48, following the Horizon Europe Guide for Proposers for Lump Sum 
Grants29.  Refer to figure 2 for a graphical presentation of the work structure components showing how they inter-
relate. The Gantt chart below shows the sequencing of the WPs and their tasks and plots deliverables and the 
project’s critical path milestones, to facilitate monitoring of technological progression & dependencies.

Write Section 3.3 Consortium as a Whole for a Horizon Europe proposal on: circular polyurethane recycling, chemical depolymerisation, and bio-based PU synthesis

consortium as a whole  
The NIPULOOP consortium is composed of a complementary set of academic, research, technology and industrial 
partners covering the full value chain required to develop and validate a circular-by-design NIPU technology 
platform. The consortium has good European coverage from Spain, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Poland, Turkey (with 
cooperation among Members States and widening countries).  
The team brings together expertise in polymer chemistry, materials processing, industrial manufacturing, 
digitalisation, circularity and recycling, regulatory compliance, and techno-economic assessment. In terms of the 
materials development, Universidad de Castilla-La Mancha (UCLM) and Łukasiewicz Research Network – Institute 
for Renewable Resources Chemistry (ICSO) lead the design & synthesis of NIPU formulations, incl. the development 
of cyclic carbonates and amine precursors from bio-based and waste-derived feedstocks such as used cooking oil 
(UCO) & CO₂. These activities are closely linked to modelling and structure–property–process relationships, 
ensuring that material design is aligned with processability and recyclability requirements from the outset. 
Process development and scale-up are led by Fraunhofer (FHG), and supported by ARCESSO, with expertise in 
polymer development and feedstock integration, supporting scale-up and industrial relevance. FHG provides 
advanced infrastructure for continuous synthesis and reactive extrusion, enabling the transition from laboratory-scale 
formulations to pilot-scale processing. ARCESSO contributes expertise and equipment in PU processing

By integrating advanced materials development with digital intelligence and circular business models, NIPULOOP 
establishes a fully digitalised circular materials workflow (applicable across key material configurations (e.g. foams, 
coatings and elastomeric systems), positioning Europe at the forefront of safe, circular and data-driven polymer 
research and development and manufacturing, reducing dependency on imported isocyanates and fossil-based 
intermediates while strengthening resilient and sustainable industrial value chains. 
Methodological Framework for achieving the project’s objectives: NIPULOOP is structured around 6 interlinked 
technological and innovation clusters that together enable the full innovation cycle from circular material design 
through industrial integration to system-level circular validation across strategic value chains. These clusters are 
strongly interconnected, with continuous feedback loops between material design, processing, digital intelligence 
and recycling, ensuring that circularity, safety and industrial feasibility are systematically reinforced throughout the 
project and collectively implemented as a coherent circular NIPU technology platform (operationalised through the 
work plan presented in Section 3.1).  
 
Figure 2. Methodological Framework 

HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-05:  
Circular innovative advanced materials: facilitating the transition from design to markets  
 
Page 9 of 48 
Cluster 1. Circular-by-Design NIPU Chemistry (TRL4–5): This cluster establishes the chemical and material 
foundations of NIPULOOP through a circular-by-design approach, in which feedstock selection, molecular 
architecture, processability and end-of-life recovery are addressed as a single, integrated design problem. The 
methodology combines renewable and secondary feedstock conversion, formulation design, and design-for-recycling 
criteria, ensuring that performance, safety and circularity are embedded from the outset. Material formulations are 
aligned with the functional and regulatory requirements of target applications, particularly for the mobility sector. 
Feedstock development and qualification: The approach will start with the preparation and qualification of 
renewable and secondary feedstocks, with particular focus on used cooking oil (UCO), CO₂-derived intermediates, 
and secondary fractions recovered from construction and demolition waste (CDW), including PU-derived amine-
rich streams. Feedstock development follows a controlled, multi-stage conversion route from waste-derived lipid 
streams to functional cyclic carbonate precursors suitable for NIPU synthesis. This includes purification, epoxidation 
and carbonation steps, each optimised to ensure reproducibility, chemical functionality and compatibility with 
downstream processing. Feedstock variability is integrated as a core methodological parameter in formulation and 
process development. Variations in fatty acid composition, impurity profiles and seasonal supply are systematically 
characterised, as these directly influence epoxide functionality, cyclic carbonate yield and ultimately NIPU network 
structure. Reaction conditions for epoxidation and carbonation are optimised to maximise functional group 
conversion while limiting side reactions, with target yields ≥85% at each stage. The resulting intermediates are fully 
characterised using spectroscopic and thermal techniques, ensuring traceability between feedstock composition and 
material performance. CDW-derived fractions are assessed as secondary feedstock sources, focusing on recovery of 
functional intermediates (e.g. amines) compatible with NIPU synthesis.
